---
title: "Setup instructions"
---

Please work through the steps below before the session. Each one takes just a few minutes.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Already set up?
If you already have Git installed, a GitHub account, and GitHub Desktop signed in, you're done.
:::

---

## Step 1 · Install Git {#install-git}

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Mac

Git is not installed by default on macOS. The easiest way to get it is to install the **Xcode Command Line Tools**:

1. Open **Terminal** (search for it in Spotlight with `Cmd+Space`)
2. Run:
   ```bash
   xcode-select --install
   ```
3. A dialogue will appear asking you to install the tools — click **Install** and wait for it to finish (a few minutes)
4. Once installed, verify it worked:
   ```bash
   git --version
   ```
   You should see a version number like `git version 2.39.3`.

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Alternative: install via Homebrew
[Homebrew](https://brew.sh) is a package manager for macOS that makes installing developer tools straightforward. If you already have it, run:

```bash
brew install git
```

If you don't have Homebrew yet, install it first from [brew.sh](https://brew.sh), then run the command above.
:::

### Windows

**On a KCL laptop?** Open the **KCL Software Center** and install both of these:

<ul style="list-style:none; padding-left:0; margin-bottom:0.8em;">
  <li style="margin-bottom:0.4em;"><img src="images/software-center-git.png" width="52" style="vertical-align:middle; margin-right:0.6em;"> <strong>Git (x64)</strong></li>
  <li><img src="images/software-center-github-desktop.png" width="52" style="vertical-align:middle; margin-right:0.6em;"> <strong>GitHub Desktop</strong></li>
</ul>

Installing both here means you can skip Step 3 below.

**On a personal device?**

1. Download **Git for Windows** from [git-scm.com/download/win](https://git-scm.com/download/win)
2. Run the installer. The default settings are fine — click through and accept them.
3. This installs both Git and **Git Bash** (a terminal for running Git commands on Windows).

Whichever route you took, verify it worked: open **Git Bash** (search for it in the Start menu) and run:

```bash
git --version
```

:::

---

## Step 2 · Create a GitHub account {#github-account}

If you don't already have one, create a free account at [github.com](https://github.com).

Use your KCL email address — it's useful for research-related repositories and may give you access to additional features via GitHub's academic programme.

::: {.callout-note}
GitHub accounts are free and personal. You can add other email addresses to your account later, and your repositories stay with you if you move institutions.
:::

---

## Step 3 · Install GitHub Desktop {#github-desktop}

GitHub Desktop gives you a visual interface for Git — you can see your changes, write commit messages, and push to GitHub without using the command line.

1. Download from [desktop.github.com](https://desktop.github.com) and run the installer
2. Open GitHub Desktop and sign in with your GitHub account

::: {#github-cli .callout-note collapse="true"}
## Command line users
If you're comfortable in the terminal and prefer to use Git from the command line, that's fine — both paths are covered in the exercises. If you plan to follow the command-line route in the exercises, install the **GitHub CLI** (`gh`) as well, because we'll use it for GitHub authentication:

- Mac: `brew install gh`
- Windows: download from [cli.github.com](https://cli.github.com)

Then run `gh auth login` to authenticate Git and GitHub on your machine. When prompted, you can choose either HTTPS or SSH for Git operations.

If you want to use SSH, GitHub has a step-by-step guide here: [Generating a new SSH key and adding it to the ssh-agent](https://docs.github.com/en/authentication/connecting-to-github-with-ssh/generating-a-new-ssh-key-and-adding-it-to-the-ssh-agent).
:::

---

## Step 4 · Configure Git {#configure-git}

This step tells Git your name and email so they appear in your commits. You only need to do this once.

Open a terminal (**Terminal** on Mac, **Git Bash** on Windows) and run these three commands, replacing the name and email with your own:

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@kcl.ac.uk"
git config --global init.defaultBranch main
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

### Mac

```bash
git config --global core.autocrlf input
```

### Windows

```bash
git config --global core.autocrlf true
```

:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What does this do?

- `user.name` and `user.email` — attached to every commit you make, so collaborators know who made each change
- `init.defaultBranch main` — new repositories will use `main` as the default branch name (the modern convention)
- `core.autocrlf` — handles line endings correctly between Mac and Windows (avoids spurious file changes)

These settings are stored in a file called `.gitconfig` in your home folder:

- **Mac/Linux:** `~/.gitconfig`
- **Windows:** `C:\Users\YourName\.gitconfig`
:::

---

## All done?

You're ready for the session. See you on **Monday 20 April at 13:30**.
